data.description:
Reporter NameCotter Jennifer Cunningham
RelationshipChief Content Officer
TypeSell
Amount$1,208,228
SEC FilingForm 4

Jennifer Cunningham Cotter, Chief Content Officer of Peloton Interactive, sold 187,238 shares of Class A Common Stock on March 6, 2025, at a weighted average price of $6.4529 per share, totaling $1,208,228. Following the transaction, Cotter directly owns 100,269 shares of the company. The sale was conducted under a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan adopted on December 5, 2024.

data.title: Materials Gain After Jobs Data - Materials Roundup
data.description:
Producers of metals and other raw materials rose after tepid jobs data and more salvos in a burgeoning trade war kept rate-cut hopes alive, despite a vow from Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell to remain patient.

"We expect that the labor market will continue to cool gradually in the months ahead, with downside risks due to government spending uncertainty," said economists at brokerage Jefferies. The Jefferies economists forecast three rate cuts this year, while cautioning that the Trump administration's unpredictable policies could trigger adjustments to the forecast.

A second round of tariffs on Chinese imports means that all goods from that nation will face at least a 20% levy at the U.S. border. China reacted in kind, levying tariffs on U.S. agricultural products, coal, energy and some manufactured goods.

Wang Yi, China's foreign minister, condemned Trump's moves as a failure to abide by trading norms, and vowed more retaliation.

Gold futures fell from record highs as traders rotated back into risky sectors from safe havens. Copper futures have strengthened recently due to the weakening dollar and optimism about Chinese economic activity.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Industrials Advance on Fed Hopes - Industrials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of industrial and transportation companies rose amid hopes that weak jobs data would spur the Federal Reserve to cut rates.

The economy added a seasonally adjusted 151,000 jobs in February, the Labor Department reported, slightly below the gain of 170,000 jobs economists polled by The Wall Street Journal expected to see.

Fed Chairman Jerome Powell said the central bank was waiting to see whether the Trump administration's trade and economic policies affected inflation and other economic trends.

The burgeoning trade war means the central bank is highly unlikely to alter its policy in the near term, according to most strategists.

Economists at brokerage Jefferies said a rate cut was likely in June, if the economy remains on its current trajectory.

French shipping magnate Rodolphe Saade, chief executive of CMA CGM, met President Trump at the White House on Thursday and pledged to invest $20 billion in the U.S. over the next four years.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Health Care Flat Amid Deal Activity - Health Care Roundup
data.description:
Health-care companies were more or less flat amid deal activity.

Walgreens Boots Alliance rose after leveraged-buyout firm Sycamore Partners agreed to buy the global drug-store chain for $11.45 a share in cash, or about $10 billion, a 29% premium.

It was a respite for Walgreens after a punishing few years during which the historic U.S. drug-store chain struggled to incorporate the purchase of British rival Boots. In less than 10 years, Walgreens strategic slips reduced it from one of the world's most valuable companies to a buyout target. Appetite for higher risk health-care issues, such as

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Energy Gains With Oil on OPEC+ Hopes - Energy Roundup
data.description:
Shares of energy companies ticked up alongside oil futures after a Russian deputy prime minister reportedly said OPEC+ could reverse plans to increase production if prices remain under pressure.

President Trump said he is "strongly considering" imposing far-reaching sanctions and tariffs on Russia until a peace agreement is reached in the war in Ukraine, as his administration pushes for a peace deal.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: DexCom Gets FDA Warning Letter After Inspections of Facilities
data.description:
By Sabela Ojea

DexCom received a warning letter from the Food and Drug Administration following inspections of its San Diego and Mesa, Ariz. facilities and is working on a written response.

The diabetes devices maker on Friday said the warning letter doesn't restrict its ability to produce, market, manufacture or distribute products. It doesn't require the recall of any products either.

In the letter the FDA mentioned non-conformities in manufacturing processes and the quality management system, the company said in a filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

DexCom said it takes the matters identified seriously and intends to continue to undertake certain corrections and corrective actions.

"Until the issues cited in the warning letter are resolved to the FDA's satisfaction, additional legal or regulatory action may be taken without further notice," the company said, adding that it can't give any assurances that the FDA will be satisfied with its response.

Write to Sabela Ojea at sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix

data.title: Cogent Biosciences Initiated at Sector Outperform by Scotiabank
data.description: (22:35 GMT) Cogent Biosciences Price Target Announced at $17.00/Share by Scotiabank

data.title: Utilities Climb, Recouping Some Losses on Rate Cut Hopes - Utilities Roundup
data.description:
Shares of power producers rose, recouping some of their recent losses amid hopes of a rate cut.

Fears that a trade war will push up inflation and, hence, Treasury yields have weighed on utility sector in recent sessions.

Utilities have a twofold rate sensitivity because the companies are typically heavily indebted and compete with bonds as dividend-oriented investments.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Tech Advances, Recouping Some Losses After Broadcom Earnings - Tech Roundup
data.description:
Shares of technology companies rose, recouping some of their recent losses following strong earnings from one major chip maker.

Broadcom shares soared after the maker of chips for artificial-intelligence applications posted quarterly earnings ahead of Wall Street targets, reflecting 77% growth in AI revenue.

Nvidia and other major chip makers rose in a relief rally. The chip sector recouped some of the losses from Thursday's rout, triggered by a revenue shortfall reported by Broadcom rival Marvell Technologies.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Sterling Gains 2.73% to $1.2923 — Data Talk
data.description:
British pound/dollar: 1.2923 dollars per British pound (0.7738 British pound per dollar)

  • This week the British pound gained 2.73% vs. the dollar
  • Largest one-week percentage gain since Friday, Nov. 11, 2022
  • Up four of the past five weeks
  • Today the British pound gained 0.32% vs. the dollar
  • Up four of the past five sessions
  • Off 13.12% since the pre-Brexit vote level of 1.49 on Thursday, June 23, 2016
  • Highest five pm New York rate since Thursday, Nov. 7, 2024
  • Off 3.67% from its 52-week high of 1.3415 hit Tuesday, Sept. 24, 2024
  • Up 6.21% from its 52-week low of 1.2167 hit Friday, Jan. 17, 2025
  • Rose 0.50% vs the dollar from 52 weeks ago
  • Year-to-date the British pound is up 3.27% vs the dollar

Data based on 5 p.m. ET values

Source: Tullett Prebon and Dow Jones Market Data

data.title: Financials Drop on Volatility Fears - Financials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of banks and other financial institutions fell amid concerns about interest rates and volatile equity markets.

Insurance giant Allianz is in exclusive talks to acquire fellow German insurance-industry services firm Viridium for more than $3 billion.

Leveraged-buyout firm Bain Capital will get a look at Insignia Financial's books after it sweetened its offer for the Australian wealth manager to about $2.12 billion, matching a bid from rival suitor CVC Capital.

Investment firm Blackstone closed on an $8 billion commercial real-estate debt fund, in the latest sign of a rebound in commercial property following a prolonged, pandemic-era slump in office occupancy.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Consumer Cos Flat on Luxury Slide - Consumer Roundup
data.description:
Consumer companies were more or less flat amid renewed signs of distress in the luxury niche.

Shares of Italian high-end fashion firm Salvatore Ferragamo tumbled as a rebranding effort faltered.

The sales disappointment heightened concerns that younger consumers in the West were favoring "fast fashion" and casual clothes over premium brands.

Shares of Louis Vuitton owner LVMH, Gucci parent Kering, Burberry and Cartier owner Richemont fell in sympathy. Historic Canadian chain Hudson's Bay is set to file for bankruptcy.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Communciations Services Climb, on Rotation Back to Risk - Communications Services Roundup
data.description:
Communications services companies rose, as traders rotated back into high-risk sectors.

Shares of Universal Music rose after the record label behind Taylor Swift posted subscription revenue growth that was ahead of consensus estimates.

ESPN anchor Stephen Smith is getting a raise, the prominent commentator and executive producer of the sports network's morning sports talk show "First Take," signed a new five-year deal with the network worth more than $100 million.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Corn futures end higher, extending rally on tariff relief
data.description:
Chicago Board of Trade corn futures rose for a third straight session on Friday as news of exemptions for Mexico and Canada to most U.S. tariffs allowed grain prices to stabilize after a plunge early this week.

Benchmark soybean declined but stayed inside of Thursday's trading ranges, while wheat futures drifted lower.

A drop in the dollar lent underlying support, making U.S. grains cheaper overseas.

CBOT May corn (CK25) settled 5-1/4 cents higher at $4.69-1/4 per bushel. May soybeans (SK25) ended down 2-1/4 cents at $10.25 a bushel and CBOT May wheat (WK25) fell 2-3/4 cents at $5.51-1/4 a bushel.

The ongoing saga with U.S. tariffs remained the focus of the market. On Thursday, U.S. President Donald Trump suspended the tariffs he imposed days earlier on most goods from Canada and Mexico, the latest twist in a fluctuating trade policy.

"It's been a wild week," said Ted Seifried, chief market strategist for Zaner Ag Hedge. "We are just really exhausted, taking a little bit of a breather," Seifried said of Friday's market moves.

Trump imposed 25% tariffs on imports from Mexico and Canada on Tuesday and fresh duties on Chinese goods, fuelling worries that an expanding trade war would threaten demand for U.S. agricultural exports.

Mexico was the largest buyer of American corn and wheat in 2024 and the No. 2 destination for U.S. soybeans after China, so averting trade barriers supported grain futures, Seifried said.

However, the U.S. is still in a trade war with top global soy buyer China. Additionally, reciprocal trade barriers and other duties are expected to take effect in the following weeks.

China bought 13.61 million metric tons of soybeans in January and February, up 4.4% from the year-earlier period, according to customs data issued on Friday.

Traders expect soy flows to slow in March after some buyers brought forward shipments in anticipation of tit-for-tat tariffs. China on Tuesday responded to the latest U.S. levies with tariffs on a range of U.S. farm goods including soybeans.

Market players await the U.S. Department of Agriculture's next monthly supply/demand report on March 11. The report will consider trade policies in place when the forecasts for grains and soybeans are issued, an agency official said on Thursday.

data.title: Workiva Executives Engage in Significant Stock Transactions
data.description:
Reporter NameRelationshipTypeAmountSEC Filing
Klindt JillEVP, CFO & TreasurerSell$475,628Form 4
Ziegler BrandonEVP, CLO, CAO & Corp SecretarySell$352,737Form 4
Hawkins Michael D.EVP, SalesSell$890,266Form 4

Jill Klindt, EVP, CFO & Treasurer of Workiva, sold 5,593 shares of Class A Common Stock on March 7, 2025, at a price of $85.04 per share, totaling $475,628. Following the transaction, Klindt directly owns 119,054 shares of the company.

Brandon Ziegler, EVP, CLO, CAO & Corp Secretary of Workiva, sold 4,115 shares of Class A Common Stock on March 7, 2025, at a weighted-average price of $85.72 per share, totaling $352,737. Following the transaction, Ziegler directly owns 103,509 shares of the company.

Michael D. Hawkins, EVP of Sales at Workiva, sold 10,421 shares of Class A Common Stock on March 7, 2025, at a weighted-average price of $85.43 per share, totaling $890,266. Following the transaction, Hawkins directly owns 61,298 shares of the company. The sale price ranged from $85.07 to $85.95.
